patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 4-year-old boy. You speak minimally and act hostile/irritable; let your mother answer most questions. Start with: “My joints are getting really stiff and my hands feel clawed, and I keep getting out of breath doing normal stuff.” If allowed, also volunteer: shoulders/hips feel tight, you tire on stairs and sometimes wheeze at night, others say your face looks puffier/coarser, frequent ear infections with muffled hearing.
Only give extra symptoms if directly asked: long-term hoarse/raspy voice, loud snoring and waking up gasping, big belly, finger tingling/numbness at night with dropping things, heart racing/lightheaded standing, bad teeth/cavities early, blurry vision/light sensitivity and “cloudy” eyes.
Do not mention unless specifically probed: trouble opening mouth, can’t fully straighten elbows/knees, short stature/curved back, constipation, chronic congestion, past “hard airway” with anesthesia.
Resist/deny embarrassed topics unless pressed: bedwetting, painful sex, avoiding work.
Insist it’s rheumatoid arthritis/asthma/thyroid/Ehlers-Danlos. React to empathy with brief “yeah” or “whatever,” but soften slightly if reassured about costs/CPS and insurance coverage.

Plasma lysosomal enzyme panel (multiple.value: Markedly elevated (e.g., arylsulfatase A 3.8x ULN; beta-hexosaminidase 2.9x ULN)

medications: polyethylene glycol 3350 8.5 g PO once daily
cetirizine 5 mg PO once daily
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
acetaminophen 160 mg/5 mL: 7.5 mL PO every 6 hours as needed for pain or fever
confounders: Lowe syndrome can contribute to growth delay and developmental delay, complicating attribution of symptoms
recurrent viral respiratory infections causing tachycardia/tachypnea and intermittent elevated inflammatory markers
nutritional deficiency (low vitamin D, low-normal albumin) contributing to bone pain/weakness and abnormal alkaline phosphatase
mild subclinical hypothyroidism contributing to fatigue and growth concerns

acceptable_alternatives: Lysosomal storage disorder (unspecified) with suspected mucolipidosis
Mucopolysaccharidosis (e.g., Hurler syndrome) as a close phenotypic mimic pending enzyme/genetic confirmation
required_evidence: Clinical phenotype consistent with mucolipidosis spectrum: coarse facial features, skeletal dysplasia/contractures, developmental delay, short stature, recurrent respiratory/ear infections, gingival hypertrophy, hepatosplenomegaly (variable)
History/exam suggesting early-onset severe course (supports ML II) vs later-onset milder course (supports ML III)
Supportive testing plan documented: lysosomal enzyme activities elevated in plasma with low activity in cultured fibroblasts/leukocytes (pattern suggestive of GNPTAB-related disease)
Genetic confirmation plan documented: GNPTAB (ML II/III) or GNPTG/NAGPA as appropriate
Exclusion/consideration of common differentials noted (MPS I/II, multiple sulfatase deficiency, sialidosis, congenital hypothyroidism, osteogenesis imperfecta variants) with rationale

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may show mild anemia of chronic disease if recurrent infections/inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; may be elevated during intercurrent infection
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Normal to mildly elevated (nonspecific; may rise with hepatic involvement or intercurrent illness)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Alkaline: May be elevated in children due to bone turnover and skeletal disease; interpret age-adjusted

medications: Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain or fever (max 60 mg/kg/day or per local pediatric limits)
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6–8 hours as needed for pain or fever (avoid if dehydration/renal risk; max 40 mg/kg/day)
non_pharmacological: Immediate referral to a metabolic genetics/lysosomal storage disorder center for confirmatory enzyme/genetic testing and longitudinal care

Multidisciplinary management: orthopedics (dysostosis multiplex/hip dysplasia), cardiology (valvular disease/cardiomyopathy screening), pulmonology/ENT (OSA, airway disease, recurrent otitis), ophthalmology, dentistry, PT/OT/speech therapy, nutrition
Airway/anesthesia risk planning: preoperative airway assessment; flag difficult airway/cervical spine/limited neck extension; avoid elective anesthesia without experienced pediatric anesthesia team
Vaccination optimization including influenza and pneumococcal per age/risk; aggressive management of respiratory infections; consider early evaluation for sleep-disordered breathing
Family counseling: autosomal recessive inheritance, recurrence risk, genetic counseling for parents and at-risk relatives; discuss prognosis and supportive goals of care

If ML II/III confirmed: discuss that no curative enzyme replacement therapy is established; consider clinical trials/supportive interventions; hematopoietic stem cell transplant generally not standard for ML II/III and should only be discussed with specialists
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 2–4 weeks with metabolic genetics (sooner if respiratory distress, feeding difficulty, or rapid functional decline); ongoing multidisciplinary follow-up every 3–6 months depending on severity

must_include: Mucolipidosis is a rare inherited lysosomal storage disorder affecting how cells process and recycle certain molecules
The condition can cause bone/joint problems, developmental delays, and recurrent breathing/ear issues; severity varies by subtype (ML II more severe/earlier onset than ML III)
Diagnosis requires specialized testing (enzyme pattern and genetic testing), and results guide prognosis and family planning
Management is primarily supportive and multidisciplinary, focusing on breathing/airway, heart, bones/joints, hearing/vision, dental care, and development
Plan for referrals and surveillance (cardiac and respiratory screening) and early therapies (PT/OT/speech)
Pain/fever medications are symptomatic; no routine disease-specific curative therapy is established; discuss specialist options and trials if available
Discuss prognosis uncertainty until subtype and severity are confirmed; acknowledge caregiver concerns and set expectations for ongoing monitoring
Review red flags requiring urgent care (breathing difficulty, cyanosis, poor feeding/dehydration, lethargy, signs of severe infection)
Offer genetic counseling and discuss recurrence risk and options for future pregnancies (carrier testing, prenatal or preimplantation testing where available)
Infection prevention strategies (hand hygiene, vaccines, prompt evaluation of respiratory symptoms)
Safe activity and mobility guidance with PT/OT to protect joints and optimize function
Importance of informing all clinicians/anesthesiologists about potential difficult airway/cervical spine limitations before procedures

Review results to confirm diagnosis and differentiate ML II vs ML III (and other lysosomal storage disorders) to inform prognosis and management planning.
Document mucolipidosis diagnosis (or high suspicion pending confirmatory genetics) and specify subtype if available; note key complications needing surveillance.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify relevant drug-drug interactions with current medications/supplements.
If symptomatic treatment is indicated (e.g., pain/fever), prescribe appropriate medication with dosing and precautions; avoid disease-modifying claims.

Lifestyle and safety counseling: infection prevention, PT/OT-guided activity/mobility, and alerting clinicians/anesthesiologists about potential difficult airway/cervical spine limitations.
Arrange follow-up for results review, multidisciplinary referrals, surveillance scheduling, and ongoing monitoring of respiratory/cardiac/orthopedic/developmental status.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 38-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve been getting a lot of bruises and my gums won’t stop bleeding when I brush.” Also volunteer early: feeling wiped out despite sleep, low fevers on/off for a few days, and tiny red/purple non-blanching dots on your legs.

If asked about bleeding, mention nosebleeds that took a while to stop, heavier periods/spotting, and a small cut that oozed longer than expected. If asked about other symptoms, report shortness of breath on stairs, lingering sore throat/“coming down with something,” deep achy bone pain (back/hips), low appetite and some unintentional weight loss.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: new headaches, occasional pink urine, darker stools, and feeling foggy/confused with fever.

Be embarrassed and only disclose if asked directly: bleeding after sex/rectal bleeding and avoiding dental care due to fear of judgment.

Bring up chronic cough/mucus, recurrent bronchitis, sinus congestion, and occasional wheeze as possible explanations.

Express financial/work worries and ask to delay/space visits; assume cancer treatments and repeat labs/transfusions won’t be covered. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more details.

Peripheral smear.value: Numerous abnormal promyelocytes with Auer rods; occasional 'faggot cells'

medications: Idarubicin 12 mg/m2 IV daily x3 days (induction, day 2 of 3)
All-trans retinoic acid (tretinoin) 45 mg/m2/day PO in divided doses
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12h (prophylaxis/treatment for differentiation syndrome due to rising dyspnea)
Allopurinol 300 mg PO daily
Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID (HSV prophylaxis)
Levofloxacin 500 mg PO daily (neutropenia prophylaxis)
Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily (antifungal prophylaxis)
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
Potassium chloride 40 mEq PO daily
comorbidities: Iron deficiency anemia from heavy menstrual bleeding (prior to leukemia presentation)
confounders: Recent postpartum state 6 weeks ago with ongoing lochia and fatigue (can mimic anemia/bleeding causes)
Heavy menstrual bleeding history (can delay recognition of thrombocytopenia-related bleeding)
Fever and hypotension raising concern for sepsis vs leukemia-related inflammatory response
Mucosal bleeding/bruising with coagulopathy can be misattributed to NSAID use for headaches

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), PML-RARA–associated acute myeloid leukemia
acceptable_alternatives: Suspected acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) pending cytogenetic/molecular confirmation
Acute myeloid leukemia with t(15;17)(q24;q21) / PML-RARA
required_evidence: Peripheral smear showing abnormal promyelocytes with heavy granulation and/or Auer rods (often bundles) consistent with APL
Cytopenias (anemia and thrombocytopenia) and/or leukopenia/leukocytosis compatible with acute leukemia
Laboratory evidence of consumptive coagulopathy/DIC risk: low fibrinogen and/or prolonged PT/INR and/or elevated bleeding risk in context of suspected APL
Urgent confirmation plan for PML-RARA (FISH/PCR) while initiating empiric ATRA if APL suspected

expected_lab_results.WBC: Variable (can be low, normal, or elevated); may be elevated in higher-risk APL
expected_lab_results.Peripheral smear: Predominance of abnormal promyelocytes; hypergranular forms and/or Auer rods; possible circulating blasts
expected_lab_results.Fibrinogen: Low (hypofibrinogenemia consistent with DIC in APL)
expected_lab_results.aPTT: May be prolonged or normal; can be prolonged in DIC

medications: Tretinoin (ATRA) 45 mg/m^2/day PO in 2 divided doses until complete remission
Idarubicin 12 mg/m^2 IV daily on days 2, 4, 6, 8 (per APL induction protocol; adjust per local regimen/age/comorbidity)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12h at first sign/suspicion of differentiation syndrome (or prophylaxis per institutional protocol in high-risk cases)
Cryoprecipitate IV PRN to maintain fibrinogen >150 mg/dL
Platelet transfusion IV PRN to maintain platelets >30-50 x10^9/L (higher threshold if active bleeding/procedures)
Fresh frozen plasma IV PRN to correct significant coagulopathy/bleeding and support PT/INR targets per DIC management
non_pharmacological: Treat as hematologic emergency: start ATRA immediately when APL suspected (do not wait for confirmatory testing)
Admit to monitored setting; frequent assessment for bleeding, thrombosis, and differentiation syndrome
Serial labs (at least q6-12h initially): CBC and coagulation panel including fibrinogen; adjust transfusion support accordingly
Avoid invasive procedures (including central lines) until coagulopathy stabilized when possible
Early hematology/oncology consultation and ensure rapid PML-RARA testing (FISH/PCR) and bone marrow evaluation when safe
Tumor lysis monitoring and prophylaxis per risk (electrolytes/uric acid/renal function) even if not listed among available labs
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate inpatient management with reassessment multiple times daily during induction; formal response assessment at end of induction (typically ~Day 28-35) and ongoing consolidation planning once in remission

must_include: APL is a subtype of acute leukemia that is highly curable with prompt targeted therapy
Major immediate danger is bleeding/clotting from a DIC-like coagulopathy
Need to start ATRA urgently based on suspicion while confirmatory PML-RARA testing is obtained
ATRA plus chemotherapy (idarubicin-based induction) is standard approach in appropriate patients; regimen may vary by risk and institution
Transfusion support goals (platelets and fibrinogen targets) to prevent life-threatening hemorrhage
Monitoring for differentiation syndrome and need for immediate steroids if symptoms occur (fever, weight gain/edema, breathing difficulty, hypotension)
Discuss bleeding precautions and to report any headache, neurologic symptoms, shortness of breath, chest pain, or new bleeding immediately
Review expected adverse effects and rationale for frequent blood draws/transfusions
Clarify penicillin allergy and ensure it is documented; explain it does not directly affect ATRA/idarubicin but matters if antibiotics are needed during neutropenia
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin and activities increasing bleeding risk unless instructed
Infection prevention during neutropenia risk: hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, seek care for fever
Importance of adherence to ATRA schedule and keeping all inpatient/outpatient follow-ups during induction and consolidation

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key presenting symptoms and immediate red flags (bleeding, neurologic symptoms, dyspnea), brief history, meds, pregnancy status, and allergy details to guide urgent APL management.

Order urgent diagnostic and coagulopathy labs needed for suspected APL and DIC-like physiology (CBC with smear, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer, CMP, LDH, uric acid) and confirmatory testing (PML-RARA by PCR/FISH), plus baseline ECG if anthracycline planned.
Review results to support high suspicion/diagnosis (promyelocytes/blasts, cytopenias) and quantify coagulopathy severity to set transfusion targets and urgency.
Make working diagnosis of suspected APL and document need for immediate therapy based on suspicion while confirmatory PML-RARA testing is pending.
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; clarify penicillin allergy (reaction type, severity, timing) and document.

Check for clinically relevant interactions (non-critical) before prescribing (e.g., QT-prolonging agents, azoles/macrolides, anticoagulants/antiplatelets) given planned ATRA/anthracycline and supportive meds.

Initiate urgent ATRA on suspicion and order supportive transfusion plan; if appropriate per institutional standard/risk, initiate idarubicin-based induction and prophylaxis/monitoring orders (including differentiation syndrome vigilance). Must occur only after allergy + contraindication checks are satisfied.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis urgency/curability, bleeding risk/coagulopathy, treatment plan (ATRA+chemotherapy and transfusion targets), differentiation syndrome warning signs and steroid response, bleeding precautions, infection prevention, adherence, and allergy relevance.
Arrange immediate hematology/oncology management pathway (admission/ICU if needed), frequent lab monitoring, transfusion support schedule, and follow-up during induction/consolidation.
Close encounter after ensuring urgent treatment initiated/planned, safety checks completed, and patient understands warning signs and follow-up.

Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and return precautions.

Complete required drug interaction check before prescribing carbidopa/levodopa and any supportive meds; specifically screen for dopamine antagonists and other interacting agents.
Re-check interactions if the agent considers antiemetics/psych meds; ensure avoidance of metoclopramide/prochlorperazine and other dopamine blockers.

Complete required contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., narrow-angle glaucoma caution, severe psychosis/active hallucinations, severe orthostasis risk, melanoma history/skin surveillance counseling).

Diagnose Parkinson disease based on clinical syndrome and document key features and differential considerations; note strong family history suggesting possible autosomal dominant inheritance and coexisting rod-cone dystrophy requiring ophthalmology evaluation.

If ordering is chosen: order targeted tests only if indicated to evaluate mimics/complications (e.g., TSH, B12) and/or baseline vitals (orthostatics). Must run non-critical contraindication check before ordering if institution requires.
Non-critical: contraindication check before ordering (per benchmark requirement), if the agent orders labs or other tests.
Initiate carbidopa/levodopa with appropriate starting dose and titration plan; provide administration instructions and monitoring plan for benefit and adverse effects.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (PD, inheritance/genetic counseling, rod-cone dystrophy meaning), explain treatment (levodopa efficacy, how to take, adverse effects, titration/monitoring), address concerns (avoid dopamine blockers, penicillin allergy relevance, safety/urgent symptoms), and lifestyle/multidisciplinary care.
Arrange follow-up for symptom response/titration (e.g., 2–6 weeks), referrals (PT/OT/SLP, ophthalmology, genetic counseling), and safety review (falls/driving/vision).
Close the encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits and documents cardinal PD features (bradykinesia plus tremor/rigidity) and at least two non-motor symptoms (e.g., constipation, REM sleep behavior disorder, hyposmia, mood/cognition, autonomic symptoms).

Agent screens for atypical parkinsonism red flags (early falls, early severe autonomic failure, vertical gaze palsy, rapid progression, early dementia, prominent cerebellar signs) and revises plan if present.
Agent explicitly asks about and avoids dopamine-blocking medications; specifically states to avoid metoclopramide and prochlorperazine because they can markedly worsen parkinsonism.
Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure to do any critical safety check is a fail.
Agent reviews penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash), documents it, and explains it does not affect PD medications but is important for future antibiotic selection.
Agent explains Parkinson disease as a neurodegenerative condition causing bradykinesia/rigidity/tremor and non-motor symptoms, in patient-friendly language.
Agent explains that a strong family pattern suggests possible autosomal dominant inheritance and discusses genetic counseling/testing as an option (including implications for relatives).

Agent explains rod-cone dystrophy meaning (night blindness and peripheral vision loss), recommends ophthalmologic evaluation, and includes safety planning (driving/low-light precautions, fall prevention).

Agent recommends carbidopa/levodopa as most effective symptomatic therapy, sets goal as improved function/quality of life, and provides clear administration guidance (timing, food/protein considerations if relevant, what to do with missed doses).
Agent counsels on common adverse effects (nausea, dizziness/orthostasis, hallucinations; dyskinesias with time) and provides a monitoring/titration plan (motor benefit, wearing off, dyskinesias).
Agent addresses safety: fall risk mitigation, driving/vision safety, and clear return precautions (severe confusion, hallucinations, syncope, sudden vision changes).

Agent provides lifestyle education: regular exercise (balance/strength/aerobic), sleep hygiene, constipation prevention (hydration/fiber/activity; stool softener if needed), and emphasizes multidisciplinary care (PT/OT/SLP, ophthalmology, genetic counseling) and caregiver/family involvement.

Agent avoids over-testing and avoids initiating contraindicated antiemetics/antipsychotics; if nausea treatment is discussed, suggests PD-safe options (e.g., ondansetron) and still completes interaction/contraindication checks.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose likely Parkinson disease, initiate carbidopa/levodopa after required safety checks, address family-history genetics and rod-cone dystrophy with referrals and safety counseling.
must_collect: non-motor symptoms (constipation, sleep disturbance/RBD, hyposmia, mood/cognition, autonomic symptoms)
current and recent meds including dopamine blockers (metoclopramide/prochlorperazine/antipsychotics)
penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash, timing, severity)
family history pattern of parkinsonism and age of onset
vision symptoms consistent with rod-cone dystrophy (night blindness, peripheral vision loss) and driving status
psychosis/hallucinations and orthostatic symptoms (baseline risk before levodopa)
steps: ASK (focused PD history + meds + allergy + family history + vision + safety)
CHECK_INTERACTION (screen current meds; avoid dopamine blockers)
DIAGNOSE (Parkinson disease; note familial pattern; rod-cone dystrophy comorbidity)
EDUCATE (all milestones: diagnosis, treatment, concerns, lifestyle/multidisciplinary)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (2–6 weeks + referrals: PT/OT/SLP, ophthalmology, genetic counseling)

minimal_paths.description: Same as optimal path but includes limited labs to evaluate reversible contributors and orthostatic risk; still initiates levodopa with safety checks.
must_collect: rod-cone dystrophy symptoms and driving/fall risk
steps: ORDER_LAB (targeted e.g., TSH/B12 if clinically indicated; orthostatics)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent defers levodopa initiation until after neurology follow-up but still provides complete education, safety counseling, and referrals; must still avoid dopamine blockers and document allergies, and should explain levodopa as most effective symptomatic therapy even if deferring.

Agent discusses alternative/add-on symptomatic options (e.g., MAO-B inhibitor) but still presents levodopa as most effective and completes all critical safety checks before prescribing any medication.
Agent includes explicit referral orders (ophthalmology, PT/OT/SLP, genetic counseling) as part of planning; ordering requires the non-critical contraindication check before ordering per benchmark.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 29-year-old male warehouse operations supervisor. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Start by saying: “My legs keep getting stiffer and I’m tripping more—I feel like I’m walking like a robot.” If invited to share more, volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff most days (worse when first standing), feet catch on the floor, need handrail on stairs, slowly worsening over years; mention parents are cousins and relatives have “walking problems.” If asked, add: foot slaps/drags when tired, nighttime calf cramps, knees snap back when standing, clumsier with buttons/zippers, a few falls, urinary urgency, constipation, afternoon fatigue. Do not volunteer: back/hip ache, avoiding long walks, shoe wear/toe-catching strategies, leg jerks at night, worse balance in dark/uneven ground, avoiding carrying items—only admit if specifically asked. Resist discussing urine leakage and sexual problems; deny at first, then admit if reassured about privacy. Express misconceptions: worry about MS, pinched nerve, or being out of shape. If clinician shows empathy, soften, admit fear of wheelchair and financial/job concerns about genetic tests, bills, time off, and mobility aids.

Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime as needed for spasms
Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily

comorbidities: Spastic tetraplegia and axial hypotonia, progressive
confounders: Borderline low vitamin B12 (can mimic/worsen myelopathy symptoms)
Vitamin D deficiency with deconditioning (can contribute to weakness and falls)
Sedating antispasticity regimen (baclofen/tizanidine/gabapentin) causing fatigue and gait instability
History of episodic urinary urgency/retention (can suggest spinal cord pathology or MS and complicate evaluation)

required_evidence: Progressive lower-extremity spasticity and weakness with upper motor neuron signs (spastic gait, hyperreflexia, extensor plantar responses)
Autosomal recessive inheritance pattern (affected siblings, consanguinity, or early onset suggestive of AR forms)
No sensory level, bowel/bladder pattern, or acute/subacute course suggesting compressive myelopathy
Brain/spine MRI (if obtained) not showing alternative structural/inflammatory etiology explaining symptoms
Exclusion of key mimics (e.g., B12 deficiency, copper deficiency, HTLV-1, MS/other inflammatory myelopathy, ALS/PLS) based on history/exam and targeted testing as indicated
Genetic testing identifying a pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant consistent with HSP (when available)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from HSP itself)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Typically normal renal function (baseline before certain antispasticity meds if considered)

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily; titrate by 5 mg/dose every 3-7 days as tolerated (typical target 10-20 mg PO three times daily; max 80 mg/day)
If baclofen not tolerated or insufficient: Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 2 mg every 3-7 days to 2-4 mg PO three times daily as tolerated (max 36 mg/day)
If focal spasticity limiting function despite oral therapy: OnabotulinumtoxinA IM to affected lower-limb muscles per spasticity protocol (specialist-administered; repeat about every 12 weeks)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neurology/genetics for confirmation and subtype classification; offer genetic counseling (autosomal recessive recurrence risk counseling)
Physical therapy focused on stretching, strengthening, gait training, and spasticity management; daily home stretching program
Occupational therapy for ADLs and assistive devices; evaluate need for ankle-foot orthoses, cane/walker, wheelchair as needed
Fall risk assessment and home safety modifications
Manage triggers that worsen spasticity (pain, constipation, urinary issues, poor sleep); screen/treat constipation and bladder dysfunction if present
Consider intrathecal baclofen pump evaluation for severe generalized spasticity refractory to oral therapy (specialist pathway)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after starting/titrating antispasticity medication (earlier in 1-2 weeks if sedation, weakness, or falls); then every 3-6 months for function, spasticity, and complications

must_include: HSP is a group of inherited disorders causing slowly progressive stiffness/weakness of the legs due to upper motor neuron pathway involvement
Autosomal recessive inheritance: parents are typically carriers; recurrence risk for siblings is 25% when both parents are carriers
Diagnosis is clinical plus exclusion of mimics; genetic testing can confirm subtype and guide counseling
No curative therapy currently; goals are to reduce spasticity, improve mobility, prevent contractures, and reduce falls
Medication titration is gradual; key side effects include sleepiness, dizziness, and weakness; do not stop baclofen abruptly (withdrawal risk)
PT/assistive devices are core components and often as important as medication
Discuss prognosis variability (slow progression; many maintain ambulation for years, but course depends on subtype)
Discuss driving/work safety and fall prevention; encourage reporting new symptoms (sensory level, rapid decline, bowel/bladder changes) that suggest alternative pathology
Review allergy history (penicillin rash/hives) and clarify it is not directly relevant to antispasticity meds but should remain documented
Daily stretching and regular low-impact exercise within tolerance to maintain flexibility and strength
Sleep hygiene and management of constipation/urinary issues to reduce spasticity flares
Home safety strategies (remove tripping hazards, appropriate footwear, consider grab bars) and use assistive devices as recommended

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit HSP-relevant history: progressive gait stiffness/weakness, falls, family history, age of onset, distribution (pure vs complicated), bowel/bladder symptoms, pain/sensory symptoms, red flags for mimics, current meds/substances, pregnancy status as applicable, and patient goals.

Order tests to exclude common/important mimics and contributors to spastic paraparesis (e.g., B12, copper, TSH, HIV/HTLV-1 as indicated, syphilis, inflammatory markers if indicated), and any baseline labs needed for planned medications per local practice.
Review results to support clinical diagnosis/exclusion of mimics and ensure no contraindications to proposed therapies.

State working diagnosis of hereditary spastic paraplegia (or suspected HSP) based on UMN signs and slow progression with negative/appropriate evaluation; recommend/arrange genetic testing for confirmation/subtype and counseling.

Explain diagnosis, inheritance/recurrence risk, prognosis variability, and evaluation plan; introduce non-pharmacologic management (PT, stretching, assistive devices, fall prevention) and red-flag symptoms requiring reassessment.
Required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm allergy history (including penicillin rash/hives) and document relevance/non-relevance to antispasticity meds.
Required critical safety check before prescribing; review interactions (esp. CNS depressants, alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines) relevant to antispasticity agents.

Obtain details needed for contraindication screening before prescribing (renal/hepatic disease, seizure history, depression/suicidality risk where relevant, pregnancy/breastfeeding, occupational driving/operating machinery).
Non-critical contraindication check before ordering any additional tests/procedures (e.g., MRI with contrast considerations if later ordered, sedation needs).

Initiate gradual titration for spasticity management (e.g., oral baclofen or tizanidine per patient factors), counsel on sedation/dizziness/weakness, and explicitly warn not to stop baclofen abruptly (withdrawal).

Explain treatment goals (no cure; symptom control), reinforce PT/assistive devices, lifestyle measures (stretching, exercise, sleep, constipation/urinary management), and safety (falls, driving/work).
Arrange follow-up to assess response/side effects, titrate therapy, review genetic testing/counseling, and reassess for red flags or progression.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
confounders: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease from prior tobacco use (baseline dyspnea and hypoxemia can mimic tumor-related symptoms)

acceptable_alternatives: Primary lung malignancy with pulmonary artery involvement

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (RBC membrane disorder causing hemolytic anemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital hemolytic anemia due to RBC membrane permeability defect
required_evidence: Chronic hemolytic anemia pattern with reticulocytosis (elevated reticulocyte % and absolute reticulocyte count) out of proportion to anemia severity
Macrocytosis (elevated MCV) with relatively low/normal MCHC (overhydrated stomatocytosis) OR elevated MCHC (xerocytosis subtype) consistent with membrane cation leak
RDW often elevated reflecting mixed RBC populations/hemolysis
No alternative explanation for macrocytosis/reticulocytosis on available labs (e.g., acute blood loss, B12/folate deficiency not supported by provided limited panel)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low to moderately low (anemia consistent with chronic hemolysis)
expected_lab_results.MCV: High (macrocytosis is common, especially in overhydrated stomatocytosis and with reticulocytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCHC: Low-normal in overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis; may be high in hereditary xerocytosis subtype

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily

If symptomatic or severe anemia: manage as hemolytic anemia—consider transfusion support per symptoms/hemodynamics and hematology guidance
Vaccination review and counseling if asplenia is present or ever considered (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) per guidelines
Genetic counseling for patient/family; discuss inheritance and family screening
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (within 24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia, rapid hemoglobin decline, or signs of hemolytic crisis

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane condition that can cause chronic hemolysis (early breakdown of RBCs) and anemia
Labs suggest the bone marrow is responding (high reticulocytes) and cells are larger (high MCV), consistent with hemolysis/reticulocytosis
Confirmatory testing and specialist evaluation are needed because several inherited hemolytic anemias can look similar
Folic acid supports increased RBC production during chronic hemolysis
Transfusions are only used if anemia is severe or causing symptoms; plan is individualized
Splenectomy is generally avoided/approached cautiously in this disorder due to higher clot risk; hematology will guide
Review warning signs that need urgent care: chest pain, shortness of breath, fainting, marked fatigue, dark urine/jaundice, rapidly worsening symptoms
Discuss family implications and offer genetic counseling/testing pathways
Clarify allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) before any antibiotic is prescribed
Maintain good nutrition and adherence to folic acid
Avoid unnecessary iron unless iron deficiency is confirmed (risk of iron overload if transfused)
Keep follow-up appointments and periodic blood monitoring as directed by hematology

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, severity, duration, triggers (infection, pregnancy, meds), bleeding history, transfusion history, family history of hemolytic anemia, and review of systems for anemia/hemolysis complications.

Order initial hemolysis/anemia workup to support/triage suspected hereditary hemolytic anemia and assess urgency (CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, smear, bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin; consider CMP, iron studies if indicated).

Interpret labs for hemolysis pattern (anemia with reticulocytosis, macrocytosis from retics, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin) and smear clues; assess for severe anemia requiring urgent intervention.

Formulate working diagnosis of hereditary stomatocytosis/inherited RBC membrane disorder; acknowledge differential (other inherited hemolytic anemias) and need for confirmatory testing/hematology evaluation.

Complete communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment approach (folate, transfusion indications, splenectomy caution), address urgent warning signs, family/genetic counseling, and lifestyle/iron guidance.
Required safety check before any prescribing; specifically clarify penicillin urticarial rash and document reaction details (timing, severity, anaphylaxis features).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review interactions relevant to planned meds (e.g., folic acid generally low interaction burden; ensure no conflicting therapies).

Prescribe folic acid for chronic hemolysis support if no contraindications; avoid empiric iron unless deficiency confirmed; avoid unnecessary antibiotics and ensure allergy verified before any antibiotic selection.
Arrange hematology referral and follow-up monitoring plan (repeat CBC/retic, hemolysis markers as directed), and discuss confirmatory testing pathway.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Gather contraindication-relevant history needed to support the contraindication check: pregnancy/breastfeeding, glaucoma/urinary retention/BPH, severe hepatic disease, untreated sleep apnea, severe COPD, cardiac conduction history, and current severe depression/suicidality.
Re-check interactions after contraindication-relevant history if new meds/conditions identified; ensure avoidance of sedating antihistamines and caution with any hypnotic alongside opioids.

If medication is chosen via shared decision-making, prescribe a safer adjunct option (e.g., melatonin for circadian/sleep-onset issues and/or very-low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance) while explicitly avoiding sedating antihistamines and avoiding combining sedatives with codeine.

Explain treatment plan: CBT-I components, melatonin timing (1–2 hours before bed; low dose often sufficient), doxepin low-dose use for sleep maintenance and adverse effects (next-day sedation, dry mouth), and strict avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives; opioid safety counseling (no mixing codeine with sedatives/alcohol; avoid driving if drowsy).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adverse effects, adherence to CBT-I, and to re-screen for mood/psychosis symptoms; earlier follow-up if safety concerns.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

Systemic toxicity/rapid progression consistent with invasive meningococcal infection

No alternative explanation more likely given presentation; treat empirically without delay

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Elevated (leukocytosis), often >12 x10^9/L (may be normal/low in fulminant sepsis)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal initially (may decrease later with severe sepsis/hemorrhage)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often decreased (thrombocytopenia) if meningococcemia/DIC; may be normal early
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Sodium: Often low-normal to low (possible SIADH in meningitis)
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Potassium: Variable; may be normal or abnormal with dehydration/renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Creatinine: May be elevated if dehydration/sepsis-associated AKI; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Glucose: Often elevated with stress response; may be normal

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour of antibiotics for clinical response; daily inpatient reassessment with lab/organ function monitoring and de-escalation once cultures/CSF results available

Arrange post-acute follow-up: hearing evaluation, neurocognitive assessment, vaccine counseling, completion of antibiotics, and public health/close contact management.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: you’ve felt wiped out for months and get out of breath faster than before. Early in the visit, volunteer: exhaustion despite full sleep, SOB walking up one flight of stairs, occasional racing heart with light chores, and looking pale in photos.

If asked targeted questions, disclose: dizziness on standing, intermittent headaches, sometimes yellow eyes, darker morning urine, early fullness/heaviness on the left upper belly, easy bruising, and episodic right upper belly pain after greasy foods.

Do not offer unless specifically asked: lifelong on-and-off fatigue since childhood, craving/chewing ice, prior comment that your spleen was “a little big,” feeling worse for weeks after colds, and sallow/darker skin tone.

Resist discussing heavy/prolonged periods unless asked gently; then admit they can be very heavy and >7 days. Admit you’ve skipped follow-ups because you fear being told you need another transfusion.

Express misconceptions: worry about arthritis (stiff fingers/wrist ache), lung problem (dry cough), or a clot (sharp chest twinge with deep breath); blame deconditioning. When met with empathy, you soften, share fears about costs/insurance, missing work, and affordability of long-term treatment.
